chunk_summary: Guidelines for discharge counseling and outpatient prescription in SAM management to reduce relapse, ensure safe feeding, and improve follow-up adherence.
learning_objectives: Understand the components of a discharge counseling checklist for SAM management.
Learn how to structure an outpatient prescription to ensure adherence and safety.
Identify danger signs requiring immediate medical attention post-discharge.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and tracing mechanisms to prevent defaulting.
key_facts: Discharge planning in SAM is a handover of risk, not an endpoint.
Outpatient prescriptions should specify therapeutic food (e.g., RUTF) with daily doses, continuation dates for antibiotics, and micronutrient schedules.
Caregivers must demonstrate correct preparation, storage, and administration of feeds and medicines before discharge.
Danger signs include poor intake, repeated vomiting, fever, hypothermia, fast breathing, convulsions, lethargy, and worsening edema.
Follow-up visits should ideally occur within 7 days, with missed visits triggering contact within 48–72 hours.
Document discharge anthropometry, edema status, and high-dose vitamin A administration to avoid duplication.
reference_quote: Discharge planning in SAM is a handover of risk, not an endpoint. (Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Caregiver education and danger sign recognition
